mem_supply.why: Severe undersupply with strong AI demand means demand exceeds supply, supporting pricing and utilization.

mem_prod.why: Speeding clean room and tool build-out is a response to strong demand and a capacity bottleneck.

mem_pricing.why: Mentions price hikes and solid pricing trend, with only a note to avoid dramatic changes.

mem_strategic.why: Multi-year demand visibility and capacity planning imply durable customer commitments.

hbm.why: HBM-linked AI demand is described as robust and part of the capacity plan, implying tight supply by demand.

gpu.why: AI accelerator revenue is expected to grow strongly, indicating sustained demand momentum.

cpu.why: CPU is increasingly part of AI infrastructure, especially for agentic workloads, lifting demand relevance.

nodes.why: Strong demand is supporting N2 ramp and multi-year capacity plans, though initial ramp does dilute margins.

cowos.why: Customers want their product demand met through front-end and high-end packaging, signaling strong packaging pull and constrained supply.

highna.why: Only references EUV supply as an issue; no clear company-specific upside or downside beyond a supply bottleneck.

gaa.why: A14 is framed as meeting the essential need for high-performance, energy-efficient computing.

ai_demand.why: Strong AI demand, robust multi-year pipeline, and severe undersupply all point to an attractive demand/supply backdrop.

agentic_ai.why: Agentic AI is described as a step-up in token consumption and CPU importance, implying rising demand.

auto.why: One cited line shows automotive down 7%, offsetting the broader longer-term capacity plan.

industrial.why: Industrial is only mentioned as a use case for added mature-node capacity, with no explicit demand strength readout.

consumer.why: Price sensitivity and memory price hikes are pressuring smartphone and PC demand, even though 3 nm interest remains healthy.

capacity.why: High utilization and ongoing capacity optimization/expansion indicate tight, well-used capacity supporting profitability.

inventory.why: Inventory rose due to 2 nm ramp and strong 3 nm demand, which looks healthy rather than bloated.

capex.why: Higher capex is being justified by customer demand and future growth, which is constructive for an equipment/capacity build cycle.

mem_demand.why: Advanced memory demand is strong and supply is expected to lag demand for the foreseeable future.

mem_pricing.why: No actual pricing commentary; the quoted material is unrelated headline noise.

mem_strategic.why: The quotes mention partnerships and strategic position, but not any specific long-term agreement terms or pricing power.

dram.why: DRAM customers are increasing EUV and immersion adoption, signaling healthy demand for ASML's tools.

nodes.why: Customers are building capacity across multiple advanced nodes and ramping 2 nm for AI, which is demand-positive for ASML's leading-edge tools.

highna.why: High-NA is progressing well, customers are testing it on real product, and ASML sees strong readiness and multi-node demand.

ai_demand.why: AI infrastructure investment is explicitly driving capacity ramps and creating strong downstream demand.

consumer.why: The quote mixes AI with mobile and PC but does not show clear standalone consumer strength for this business.

capacity.why: Customers are asking for more shipments and ASML is expanding output to support demand, which is constructive.

capex.why: Customer capital expenditure is rising to accelerate capacity ramps, which is positive for ASML's equipment demand.

china.why: ASML says it can accommodate potential export-control outcomes, suggesting manageable rather than improving or worsening conditions.

mem_demand.why: Broad-based DRAM/HBM/NAND demand is rising, with AI adding incremental memory pull.

mem_prod.why: Clean-room space and fab capacity are a pacing constraint, but new capacity is coming online to support more wafer starts.

mem_pricing.why: Management points to value-based pricing and a stronger market environment, implying pricing power.

mem_strategic.why: Rising demand and longer visibility from customers support multi-year revenue stability.

dram.why: AI-driven DRAM spending is strong and Applied says it is well positioned for the next node transitions.

hbm.why: HBM is tied to strong AI demand and packaging activity, with Applied seeing strong positions there.

nand_qlc.why: Bit growth is being raised, but new wafer starts are limited and upgrades are meeting demand.

compute_demand.why: Customers are seeing compute demand grow very quickly and across a broader base.

gpu.why: Panel-level technologies for AI accelerators are being expanded, signaling healthy accelerator demand.

cpu.why: Agentic AI is adding incremental CPU demand alongside memory demand.

nodes.why: Multi-node co-innovation and future design wins imply healthy demand for advanced-node equipment.

cowos.why: AI-driven advanced packaging spending is accelerating and shifting toward Applied's leadership positions.

gaa.why: Gate-all-around adoption is expanding the market and driving multiple product wins.

ai_demand.why: AI is explicitly described as enabling growth, higher velocity, and more design wins across the business.

agentic_ai.why: Meaningful growth in agentic applications is adding a clear tailwind to wafer fab equipment demand.

capacity.why: Customers are adding capacity aggressively, especially in DRAM and leading-edge nodes.

leadtimes.why: Just indicates suppliers need longer lead time to invest; no clear demand or supply signal.

inventory.why: Lean-out in customer inventories is constructive, though Applied is building its own inventory.

capex.why: Strong WFE growth and continued cloud capex are supportive for equipment demand.

china.why: China is expected to be flat to slightly higher, with no clear easing or worsening trend.

mem_demand.why: AI is accelerating NAND bit demand, driving greenfield investment and higher layer-count demand.

mem_prod.why: Clean room space and NAND capacity are constrained, so incremental productivity can unlock output.

mem_pricing.why: They mostly ask about pricing; no actual memory pricing trend is discussed.

dram.why: Record DRAM revenue and transition to 1C/DDR5/LPDDR5 point to strong spending and ramps.

hbm.why: HBM was 'so hot' and investments remained strong, indicating robust demand with tight supply/ramp intensity.

cowos.why: Advanced packaging demand and revenue growth are both strong, with growth expected above 50%.

consumer.why: Data center bits are larger than PC and mobile combined, signaling relatively weak consumer demand.

capacity.why: Greenfield investment and constrained fabs/clean rooms indicate supply is tight because demand is outrunning capacity.

leadtimes.why: Stretching lead times reflect strong demand and tight supply for tools.

inventory.why: Inventory turns improved to a four-year high, showing lean and healthy inventory.

mem_strategic.why: Strategic partnerships to expand compute capacity lock in future demand.

cpu.why: Management describes a large and growing CPU opportunity, including a new growth driver in Vera.

highna.why: Only references software libraries; no direct read on High-NA EUV demand or supply.

consumer.why: Consumer demand fell modestly due to higher memory and system prices.

capacity.why: Adding significant capacity for Anthropic and being supply constrained suggests demand outruns supply.

mem_prod.why: Production expansion is being intentionally accelerated via new cleanroom and fab buildouts to meet strong demand.
